name: Art Davis
abstract: Arthur David Davis (December 5, 1934 – July 29, 2007) was a double-bassist, known for his work with Thelonious Monk, John Coltrane, Dizzy Gillespie, McCoy Tyner and Max Roach.

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Dizzy Gillespie: Genius at Midnight
20th Century Innovations: Early Experiments with Jazz

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Watrous
about_quotes.evidence: And we had Jake Hanna on drums, Jim Hall on guitar and Art Davis on bass and of course Mort Lindsey on piano.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bill Watrous interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, March 26, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Alvin Queen
about_quotes.evidence: I did my first album, I was twelve years old, under the direction of Joe Newman, Zoot Sims, Art Davis and Hank Jones
about_quotes.doc_title: Alvin Queen interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, August 23, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Jake Hanna
about_quotes.evidence: Art Davis, bass, myself, and Mort Lindsay was the band leader.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jake Hanna interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: The [law]suits that went down with Arthur Davis – Art Davis and others – really bore no real fruit beyond some band-aids at the time.
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Richard Wyands
about_quotes.evidence: There were two bass players that showed up. Ray Brown was one of them, and Art Davis was the other.
about_quotes.doc_title: Richard Wyands interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 7, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Harold Mabern
about_quotes.evidence: Dr. Art Davis, psychiatrist; ... The list just goes on on on you know.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harold Mabern interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, March 31, 2010
